coredata.dc:title: Fuel waste to fluorescent carbon dots and its multifarious applications
coredata.dc:creator: Venkatesan, Srinivasan
Mariadoss, Asha Jhonsi
Arunkumar, Kathiravan
Muthupandian, Ashokkumar
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report the fluorescent carbon dots (KCDs) from kerosene fuel soot via simple one pot oxidative acid treatment. The obtained KCDs were thoroughly characterized by pivotal techniques. The prepared KCDs are 5 nm sized quasi-spherical particles and exhibited an outstanding stability against pH, NaCl and light irradiation. Intriguingly, the KCDs display an excitation wavelength independent emissive property. Further, the KCDs were successfully utilized for multifarious applications such as photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and sensing studies. For PET studies, charged porphyrins (tetra pyridyl and tetra sulphonato) were selected and the fluorescence of associated porphyrin was quenched and the quenching is due electron transfer. The lower recombination rate obtained from transient absorption spectral studies suggests that KCDs can be used as electron shuttling material in a cascade system of photovoltaic device. On the other hand, the fluorescent nature of KCDs was used for the selective and sensitive detection of explosives and metal ions. The detection limit for picric acid, Cu2+ and Fe3+ is found to be 86 ng/ml, 15.3 ng/ml and 0.36 μg/ml respectively. Moreover, the unreacted soot during the acid treatment has been calcined and used as an affordable filter bed for waste water purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Kerosene soot
PET
Explosives
Metal ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Identification of picric acid and metal ions in Real samples
Stability study of prepared carbon dots
Sensitive and selective detection of picric acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Image of kerosene soot collected (left) and final dried product of KCDs powder (Right).
HR-TEM image of KCDs (a) 20 nm scale, (b) 10 nm scale (c) Histogram plot drawn from HR-TEM of 20 nm scale and (d) Powder XRD patterns.
a) XPS survey spectrum and b) the FT-IR spectrum of prepared KCDs.
a) The absorption spectrum b) excitation independent emission spectra and c) fluorescence decay of KCDs.
Absorption spectrum of TMPyP (1 × 10−6 M) with various concentrations of KCDs.
Fluorescence spectrum of TMPyP (1 × 10−6 M) with various concentrations of KCDs. Excitation wavelength is 420 nm).
Time resolved fluorescence decay of TMPyP (1 × 10−6 M) in the absence and presence of KCDs.
(a) Transient absorption spectrum and (b) decay of TMPyP/KCDs monitored at 490 nm.
a) Fluorescence quenching of KCDs with different concentration of PA (0–10 μM) in aqueous solution and b) the linear relationship between 1-I/I0 and the concentrations of PA.

a) Comparison of fluorescence quenching efficiency of different nitro compounds on KCDs fluorescence and b) detection of PA in various real water samples. Concentrations of nitro compounds = 20 μM each.

a) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of various metal ions on KCDs fluorescence in a) water medium, b) TRIS buffer, pH = 7.0 and c) NaAc-HAc buffer, pH = 7.0. Concentrations of metal ions = 20 μM each.

Fluorescence emission spectrum of KCDs in presence of a) Cu2+ in TRIS buffer (0.1 M, pH = 7.0), c) Fe3+ in NaAc-HAc buffer solution (0.1 M, pH = 7.0) and the linear relationship between (1-I/I0) Vs concentrations of b) Cu2+ and d) Fe3+ ions.

(a) Image of the byproduct/undigested soot calcined at 400 °C for 4 h and b) Image of water collected via filtration, left (normal tissue paper as filter) and right (tissue with calcined product as filter bed).
Comparison of sensing performance of different method-based fluorescence sensors for PA detection.
Results for the detection of PA spiked with different real water samples.
Comparison of sensing performance of different method-based fluorescence sensors for Cu2+ detection.
Comparison of sensing performance of different method-based fluorescence sensors for Fe3+ detection.
Results for the detection of multi-metal ions in spiked with different real water samples.

head.ce:title: Fuel waste to fluorescent carbon dots and its multifarious applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, B. S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: B. S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology, Avadi, Chennai, 600 062, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, University of Melbourne, VIC, 3010, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report the fluorescent carbon dots (KCDs) from kerosene fuel soot via simple one pot oxidative acid treatment. The obtained KCDs were thoroughly characterized by pivotal techniques. The prepared KCDs are 5 nm sized quasi-spherical particles and exhibited an outstanding stability against pH, NaCl and light irradiation. Intriguingly, the KCDs display an excitation wavelength independent emissive property. Further, the KCDs were successfully utilized for multifarious applications such as photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and sensing studies. For PET studies, charged porphyrins (tetra pyridyl and tetra sulphonato) were selected and the fluorescence of associated porphyrin was quenched and the quenching is due electron transfer. The lower recombination rate obtained from transient absorption spectral studies suggests that KCDs can be used as electron shuttling material in a cascade system of photovoltaic device. On the other hand, the fluorescent nature of KCDs was used for the selective and sensitive detection of explosives and metal ions. The detection limit for picric acid, Cu2+ and Fe3+ is found to be 86 ng/ml, 15.3 ng/ml and 0.36 μg/ml respectively. Moreover, the unreacted soot during the acid treatment has been calcined and used as an affordable filter bed for waste water purification.

ce:section.ce:para: The majority of energy needs of humans is met by burning fossil fuels, which leads two major threats to the environment such as green house effect and ozone layer depletion causing global warming, acid rain, etc [1,2]. Regulatory authorities in most countries are seriously concerned with the emission of soot to the atmosphere by various activities. Inhalation of such soot causes many adverse health effects such as lung inflammation, cardiovascular problems and defects in brain activity, etc [3,4]. Therefore conversion of waste soot into value added product is one of the recent scenario among the research community. In other area of contemporary research is the design and synthesis of nanomaterials for diverse fields. Nanomaterials [5] are of great interest owing to their distinctive optical and other functional characteristics which direct them for potential applications in electronic as well as biological fields [6,7]. Among the numerous types of nanomaterials reported, carbon dots (CDs) are emerged as potential competitor to other conventional semiconducting nanomaterials [8]. CDs are characteristically nanomaterials consist of ill-defined nanocrystalline cores with sp2 hybridised carbon sheets (graphite/graphene/graphene oxide) merged by three dimensional sp3 carbon insertions [9]. They were revealed by Xu et al. in 2004 while purifying carbon nanotubes [10]. Generally both top-down [10–12] and bottom-up [13–16] methodologies were known to produce CDs. Notably, a green synthetic approach, under bottom-up method, was introduced by Li et al. in which ethanol was electrochemically treated in NaOH medium for the synthesis of CDs [17]. Thereafter, numerous reports for the usage of inexpensive precursors for the production of CDs followed by various protocols were developed [18–24]. In this juncture, we have also derived the CDs from green resources such as tamarind and centella asiatica via one pot hydrothermal method [25,26]. However, few drawbacks like diversity in particle size, costly processing, small scale production and poor chemical stability were faced while using green resources as precursor [27–29].

Conversion of waste biomass or pollutant into CDs is a contemporary in research which has been kicked off by Lee and his research group [30]. They have introduced bulk synthesis of CDs from food waste via ultrasonication method. Similarly, a few reports were presented for the conversion of soot collected by burning candle, natural gas etc into CDs for biological applications [31,32]. Based on this context, our ultimate aim is to convert a waste material such as kerosene fuel soot into value added product like nanostructured CDs for the effective utilization in the field of photovoltaic as well as sensing fields.

The usage of carbon based materials in photovoltaic and sensing applications are growing interest [33–37]. For instance, in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSCs) integration of graphene sheets into TiO2 photoanodes gave five times higher power conversion efficiencies than those obtained from TiO2 photoanodes without graphene sheets [36]. Imahori et al. reported the efficient hierarchical electron transfer cascade system on a semiconducting electrode by using rGO sheet to anchoring porphyrins/ZnO hybrid materials and that cascade system exhibited remarkably high photocurrent generation with an incident-photon-to-current efficiency (IPCE) of up to 70% compared with the reference device without rGO sheets [30]. Kamat et al. reported the CdSe–graphene composites in quantum dot sensitized solar cells display improved photocurrent response (∼150%) over those prepared without GO [37]. Therefore, it is significant to study the interactions between light harvesting materials and carbon nanomaterials. For interaction studies, porphyrin was selected due to its higher efficiency in dye sensitized solar cells over than other dyes [38–40]. The selected porphyrins were meso-tetrakis (1-methyl-4-pyridinio) porphyrin [TMPyP] and meso-tetrakis(4-sulfonatophenyl) porphyrin [TSPP] shown in Scheme S1.

On the other hand, CDs were highly utilized due to their fluorescence property and water soluble in nature. For instance, Wu et al. developed simple amine-capped C-dots for the selective detection of picric acid (PA) in water medium and explored that there is a strong electrostatic interaction between the amine capped CDs and PA [41]. Liu and co workers were reported, the high fluorescence quantum yield of CDs capped with BPEI using bamboo leaves as carbon source. The prepared CDs were utilized for the sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ in real water sample [42]. For the sensitivity of Fe3+, the blue emitting CDs were developed from citric acid and ethylene diamine via a hydrothermal method. The mechanism of sensing was due to complex formation between Fe3+ ion and phenolic hydroxyl groups (−OH) on the CDs surface leads electron transfer from excited state CDs to unfilled d orbital of Fe3+ ion [43]. These milieu are tempted us to utilize the KCDs in PET and sensing studies.

In the present work, we have successfully converted the waste soot collected from the kerosene fuel burning into value added product namely carbon dots (KCDs). Since it is reported that CDs have wide range of applications, herein we have utilized the prepared KCDs in multi-target applications such as PET studies with porphyrins in view of usage of KCDs as electron shuttling mediator in cascade system of photovoltaic device and the fluorescent nature of KCDs is effectively used for the selective and sensitive detection of explosives and heavy metal ions. The sensing studies have also been tested in real water sample in order to prove the practical applicability of the analysis. On the other hand, the unreacted soot has also been applied as an affordable filter bed for waste water purification. Therefore, the present manuscript unambiguously gives the knowledge of effective usage of the prepared nanomaterial (Waste to Nano) for multifarious applications.

Kerosene was purchased from a local provisional store. Porphyrins, [meso-tetrakis (1-methyl-4-pyridinio) porphyrin and meso-tetrakis (4-sulfonatophenyl) porphyrin] were procured from Sigma–Aldrich. Analytical grade sodium carbonate and nitric acid were acquired from LOBA Chemicals (India). Picric acid and other nitro aromatic compounds were purchased from Merck. ZnCl2, NiCl2.6H2O, CuCl2.2H2O, MgCl2.6H2O, CoCl2.6H2O, BaCl2.2H2O, PbCl2, FeCl2, NaCl, KCl, MnCl2.4H2O and FeCl3 were purchased from LOBA Chemicals. For spectral measurements samples were prepared by using Milli-Q water, sodium acetate and tris buffer medium.

Carbon dots from kerosene soot are synthesized by a simple one pot refluxion in nitric acid medium (oxidative acid treatment) by a reported method with some modifications [44]. A typical procedure for the synthesis is as follows: kerosene soot was made to deposit on a piece of glass plate kept on a burning lamp containing kerosene as a fuel and scrapped out. 1 g of kerosene soot collected was added to 200 mL of 5 M nitric acid in a 500 mL two-necked round bottom flask. It is observed that the soot was floating on the acid solution at room temperature which was then refluxed at 100 °C for 12 h under constant magnetic stirring. Subsequently the refluxed mixture was kept aside without disturbance to cool down to ambient temperature naturally. It is observed that the reaction mixture turned to dark brown in colour with black precipitation at the bottom which is further centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 30 min. Centrifugation was carried out to separate out the bulkier unreacted/partially digested soot materials and the clear dark brown supernatant was collected, neutralized by Na2CO3, and dialyzed against Millipore (18.2 MΩ cm) water via dialysis membrane tube (1000 MWCO) for 24 h. The process of dialysis was necessary to remove the small molecules (dialysis water is changed for every 6 h). The dialyzed clear dark brown solution contained fluorescent carbon dots (KCDs) were collected by vacuum distillation and dried under vacuum. The attracting characteristic of this method is that there is no post-synthetic surface passivation is implemented to introduce functional groups or to impart fluorescent nature and water solubility etc. Refluxion of soot in aqueous nitric acid imparts the oxidation of surface carbons into carboxylic acids, thus making the KCDs become negatively charged and hydrophilic in nature.

The detection of explosive was carried out by examining the fluorescence quenching nature of carbon dots solution in the presence of nitro explosive materials at room temperature using fluorescence spectrometer. Initially, 3 ml of diluted KCDs solution was prepared in water medium and then the specific nitro compounds solution was added sequentially. Each measurement was replicated thrice for reproducibility check.

Caution: Nitro compounds were handled with care and the dilute solutions were prepared inside a fume hood with worn gloves and goggles. The disposal of nitro compounds after analysis has also been done with utmost care including proper dilution. The storage container was kept on frequent checking such as cleaning of the bottle neck, cap and thread with a wet cloth before recapping and seal cap with Parafilm.

In general, the test solutions consisted of KCDs and various amounts of metal ions (Fe3+or Cu2+) in matching buffer solutions (Fe3+ in HAc-NaAc and Cu2+ in TRIS buffer solution) is prepared. The emission spectra of the test solutions were observed at room temperature. The signal output value was calculated according to the relative fluorescence intensity (1-(I/I0), in which I0 and I are fluorescence intensities of KCDs at 510 nm with and without the addition of corresponding metal ions, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of picric acid and metal ions in Real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to calculate the practicability of KCDs-based chemosensor for the detection of heavy metal ions and picric acid in an simulated system, ultrapure water (UW), pond water (PW), river water (RW) and lake water (LW) samples were collected from nearby water bodies. The collected water samples were initially filtered via microfiltration in order to remove the suspended particles if any and spiked with different amounts of the corresponding metal ions and nitro compounds, respectively.

Fig. 1d shows the powder XRD pattern of the KCDs which exhibits sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ = 28°, 32° and also show many small diffraction peaks at 2θ = 38°, 42°, 48°, 63° and 67° are endorsed to carbon atoms arranged in a considerably unsystematic fashion. This clearly instigate that the existence of more oxygen containing groups on the surface of KCDs make it as compactly crowded by alkyl chains and graphitic carbon. The existence of both slightly amorphous as well as crystalline arrangement of atoms on KCDs further supported by excitation independent emission behavior of KCDs (Fig. 3
b). Followed by, XPS measurements were carried out to analyze the composition and surface state of KCDs. The survey scan of XPS spectrum (Fig. 2a) depicts four prominent signals of C1s (42.25%), O1s (40.18%), N1s and Na1s (7% each) were located at 285, 533, 408, and 504 eV respectively. Among the four prominent features, we are interested in C1s and O1s peaks due to KCDs is mainly consist of these two elements in higher proportions. The other two elements such as N and Na originated from the acid treatment used for functionalization (HNO3) and Na2CO3 used for neutralization process respectively. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum (Figure S1a) reveals four discrete peaks located at 285, 286.5, 288.5 and 289.9 eV, which are attributed to CC, COC, CO and OCO groups, respectively. Likewise the characteristic O1s deconvoluted spectrum (Figure S1b) was resolute into three peaks at 531.5, 532.9 and 535.9 eV, which are recognized to CO, COH and COC groups respectively. The FT-IR spectrum of KCDs is shown in Fig. 2b which instigate that the presence of peak positions at 1795 and 3410 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibrations of carbonyl (CO) and hydroxyl (−OH) groups, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 1330 cm−1, 829 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibrations of sp2 and sp3 CH and the peak located at 1593 cm−1 corresponds to the CC stretching vibration. The 2437 cm−1 peak is assigned for NO3 − stretching vibration [45]. At the end, FT-IR spectrum clearly revealed that the presence of rich oxygen-containing groups (carboxyl, carbonyl and hydroxyl) on the surface of KCDs and unsaturated carbon were formed during the acid refluxion process [26]. Negative value of −21.2 mV from the Zeta potential analysis (Figure S2) exposed that the existence of negatively charged carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of KCDs, which further reveals that KCDs are hydrophilic in nature which imparts the excellent water solubility. The 1H NMR spectrum discloses the occurrence of four kinds of chemical environments in the three dissimilar regions (Figure S3). The signals at 1–2 ppm (for sp3 CH protons), 3–5 ppm (for the protons attached with hydroxyl, ether and carbonyl groups) and 7–8.5 ppm (for the aromatic or sp2 protons and aldehydic protons) were found which clearly illustrate the range of functional groups present on the KCDs surface. The 1H NMR results are consistent with available reports in the literature [46]. Based on the above material characterizations, we conclude that KCDs are quasi-spherical and hydrophilic in nature consisting of various functional groups on its surface.

Fig. 3a shows the absorption spectrum of KCDs. The peak at 280 nm is due to π-π* transition and a shoulder at 330 nm is corresponds to n-π* transition. Typically, CDs shows an excitation dependent emission due to a variety of defects present on the surface [47,48]. Surprisingly, in this report KCDs exhibits an excitation-independent emission maximum at 510 nm while excitation is varied from 300 to 360 nm (Fig. 3b). Ding et al. also reported the excitation-independent emission of the CDs and they hypothesized that non-radiative transitions are likely to occur [49]. Based on the report, we proposed a similar mechanism for the emission property of KCDs. The emission quantum yield (φ) of KCDs is ∼3% which was calculated with the comparison of standard quinine sulphate. The value of emission quantum yield is comparable with the reported value for CDs [50]. The emission decay of KCDs is shown in Fig. 3c. The decay is fitted with multiexponential function [F(t) = A1 exp (-t/τ1) + A2 exp (-t/τ2) + A3 exp (-t/τ3)] and the fitted values are τ1 = 0.56 ns (18%), τ2 = 1.78 ns (35%) and τ3 = 5.21 ns (47%). Multiexponential decay with three different lifetimes confirms the existence of various fluorescence states on the surface of carbon dots. The average lifetime of KCDs is calculated as τ = 3.17 ns. Such a longer lifetime drives the KCDs as the fluorescence probe as well as excited state electron transfer studies for diverse applications.

It is well documented in the literature that, the primary process in a cascade device is electron transfer [34]. Hence studying the electron transfer process between porphyrin and KCDs is of interest to further develop the cascade device on this material. Before investigating the electron transfer studies of porphyrin with KCDs, it is necessary to analyze the nature of the interaction between them in the ground state. Therefore, we employed UV–vis absorption spectroscopic technique to characterize the porphyrin/KCDs system. The absorption spectra of TMPyP with successive additions of KCDs in water are displayed in Fig. 4 . Upon increasing the concentration of KCDs, the characteristic Soret band absorbance was steadily decreased and an isosbestic point was also observed at 433 nm. The appearance of isosbestic point clearly exposed that the porphyrin exists in two different states such as adsorbed porphyrin and free porphyrin. The interaction between TMPyP and KCDs is due to electrostatic in nature, since KCDs surface has negative charges (cf. above) and the used porphyrin is positive charge (Scheme S1). To further demonstration of electrostatic interaction, we have performed the interaction studies with negatively charge porphyrin (TSPP) at the similar experimental conditions (Figure S4). Results showed that there is no interaction between TSPP and KCDs. This result is very supportive for the electrostatic interaction of the above system. Further, Raman spectral analysis has been used to provide clear evidence for interaction between TMPyP and KCDs. The recorded spectra of free TMPyP and TMPyP-KCDs mixtures are displayed in Figure S5a&b. If the dye molecule interacts with the surface of nanomaterials, there is a significant change in Raman spectra was generally observed [51]. The positively charged pyridine ring of porphyrin plays an important role for the electrostatic interaction with KCDs surface. In TMPyP, the band at 1306, 1066 cm−1 were assigned to the bending of pyridine ring. In which the former one is shifted to higher frequency and the later one was completely disappeared in the spectrum of TMPyP in presence of KCDs. Moreover in the region of 1443 and 1679 cm−1 there are new band appearance and shift was observed which were assigned to D band of sp2 and G band of sp3 hybridized bonds in KCDs. The results from Raman analysis is clearly evident that the interaction between TMPyP and KCDs is occurred via positively charged pyridine moiety of porphyrin and negatively charged surface functional groups of KCDs. On the other hand, no considerable change in the Raman spectra is observed for the TSPP with KCDs (Figure S6).

Further, the excited state interaction of porphyrin with KCDs was monitored by using steady state and time resolved fluorescence quenching measurements. We have noticed that a gradual reduction in the fluorescence intensity of TMPyP while raising the concentration of KCDs (Fig. 5 ). This result clearly suggests that there is a quenching in the singlet state of porphyrin. Therefore, the time correlated single photon counting technique has been employed to probe the singlet state quenching. Fig. 6 shows the fluorescence decay of TMPyP in water and in the presence of KCDs. The fluorescence decay of TMPyP in the absence of KCDs is fitted with single exponential function [F(t) = A exp(−t/τ)] and the lifetime is 9.6 ns. However in the presence of KCDs, the decay is quenched and fitted with bi-exponential function [F(t) = A1 exp(−t/τ1) + A2 exp(−t/τ2)], with short-lived (0.28 ns) and longer-lived (8.33 ns) components. We assigned that the short-lived component as adsorbed state and long-lived component for non-adsorbed state, based on the results from absorption spectral studies. As expected, the fluorescence decay of TSPP is not altered by KCDs (Figure S7). It is well-known that the reduction in fluorescence decay is completely due to the electron transfer process [52]. So, the rate of electron transfer (ket) from excited state TMPyP to KCDs has been calculated using Eq. (1)
(1) ket = (1/τads) − (1/τfree)
where τfree & τads are the lifetimes of porphyrin in the absence and presence of KCDs respectively and the rate of electron transfer (ket) is 3.45 × 109 s−1. The calculated ket is similar to previously reported ket for interaction studies with carbon nanomaterials [52]. Such an estimate of the ket provides the basis for using carbon nanomaterials for collecting photogenerated electrons in the cascade system [34].

The radical cation of porphyrin could be detected if the electron injection happened from the excited state of porphyrin to the KCDs. Therefore, the nanosecond transient absorption spectroscopic technique was employed to probe the radical cation of TMPyP. Fig. 7 a shows the transient absorption spectrum of TMPyP/KCDs at various time delays after excitation at 355 nm. The spectrum has a distinct band at 490 nm is ascribed to the radical cation of TMPyP in agreement with the earlier observations [53]. The rate constant for electron recombination (krec) has been determined by monitoring decay at 490 nm ( Fig. 7b) and is found to be 4.4 × 105 s−1. Intriguingly, the rate of electron–cation recombination is five orders of magnitude lower than electron injection rate, which clearly reveals that electron injection is the dominant process in TMPyP/KCDs. This further demonstrates that TMPyP/KCDs have a larger electron cation separation and this material can be used in the photovoltaic devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability study of prepared carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Before using KCDs to sensing applications, it is necessary to check the stability by various parameters such as effect of pH, ionic strength, light irradiation time and storage time on the fluorescence intensity of KCDs. Figure S8a shows the effect of pH from 1–14 on the fluorescence intensity of KCDs. The maximum emission intensity is observed at ∼ 6–7 pH. At the same time if the solution is more alkaline (pH above 10) and more acidic (pH below 3) then the fluorescence intensity of KCDs is considerably varied. This result clearly reveals that the prepared KCDs is sensitive with pH of the medium. At the same time the emission intensity of KCDs is highly stable at the pH range of 3–10 which is a good sign to employ the KCDs for sensing as well as biological applications. Further, the effects of NaCl concentration, light irradiation and storage time on the fluorescence intensity of KCDs were also investigated (Figure S8 b–d). The fluorescence intensity of KCDs is not much varied by adding NaCl (0.1–1 M), light irradiation time from 0 to 2 hrs and storage time (0–3 months), which clearly reveals that the prepared carbon dots are capable of outstanding stability and tolerance without any post stabilization treatments. The above mentioned studies help to prove the prepared KCDs can be used towards sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of picric acid
ce:section.ce:para: Principally, picric acid (2,4,6-trinitrophenol) is significantly used in the fields of insecticides, crackers, military explosives, etc [54]. It is necessary to detect the level of picric acid (PA) in real water samples, which could leach in to soil/water resources leading contamination and pollution of the environment [55]. Based on the environmental quality standards the limit of concentration of PA in portable water is only 5 mg/L and if the level increased that may cause number of health issues to the human beings as well as other living species [56]. In this context, the KCDs are used to detect the explosives due its excellent water solubility and fluorescence property. Fig. 8 a shows the fluorescence quenching spectrum of KCDs with various concentrations of PA. As observed in this figure, the fluorescence of KCDs is significantly quenched with PA concentration in the range of 0–10 μM. There is a good linear relationship between 1 − I/I0 and the concentration of PA in the range of 0 to 5 μM (Fig. 8b), which has been used to calculate the limit of detection (LOD). LOD was calculated as 86 ng/ml by using from the slope of linear regression equation (1 − I/I0 = 0.065C + 0.005) substituted in 3σ/k, in which ‘σ’ is standard deviation and k is the slope. Table 1 shows the detection limit of PA using different materials through various methods [57–61] of detection which enlightens that PA can be detected by KCDs with high sensitivity in the nanogram scale using the facile fluorescence technique. Similar measurements were performed with other nitro compounds such as o-nitrophenol (o-NP), p-nitrophenol (p-NP), 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP), nitrobenzene (NB), dinitrobenzene (DNB), 2,4-dinitrophenyl hydrazine (2,4-DPH) and phenol (PHE). The bar diagram shown in Fig. 9 a clearly demonstrates the quenching efficiency of nitro compounds with KCDs. The order of efficiency is as follows, PA > 2,4-DNP > p-NP > o-NP ≅ NB ≅ DNB ≅ 2,4-DPH ≅ PHE which is due to the order of increase in acidity of these nitro compounds. Among all the nitro compounds studied, those consist of nitro group in para position exhibit the interaction with KCDs. Here, the selective detection of PA by KCDs is shown by the higher magnitude of quenching efficiency of PA towards the fluorescence intensity of KCDs. These results are in good agreement with earlier report [62]. The effect of interference of other nitro compounds on the fluorescence quenching of KCDs by picric acid is shown in Figure S9 which clearly enlighten that the quenching efficiency of PA is not interrupted by other nitro compounds. This proves that PA can be selectively detecting from the mixture of others nitro compounds by fluorescent analysis using KCDs.

In view of practical applicability, we have conducted the detection of PA in different kind of water samples such as, river water (RW), pond water (PW) and lake water (LW) collected from nearby areas. These water samples were filtered via micro syringe filter in view of removing the minute suspended/floating materials. The water samples spiked with PA were effectively quenched the fluorescence of KCDs. The bar diagram in Fig. 9b shows similar quenching efficiency of 20 μM of PA to KCDs fluorescence in all the three types of water sample. The percentage of quenching is almost comparable with PA spiked in ultra pure water (UW) which indicates that PA present in any type of water with various contaminants can be detected (detection can be done even in the presence of other interferences in a testing water samples). Table 2 shows the quantification of PA. This observation is evident that the KCDs can be a promising fluorescent probe for detecting/quantifying PA in different kind of real water samples.

The high sensitivity of KCDs towards PA may either be due to excited state energy transfer from the KCDs to the PA or re-absorption of the emitted light of KCDs by the PA (inner filter effect). For the former case, there should be an efficient overlap between emission spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor [63]. For the latter case, there should be a good spectral overlap between the absorption band of the absorber and fluorophore. In some cases, non-specific electrostatic interactions such as hydrogen bonding may also cause the quenching effect. The possibility of electron transfer and resonance energy transfer has been ruled out based on the unaltered fluorescence decay of KCDs in presence of PA, shown in Figure S10a. The excitation and emission spectra of KCDs with absorption spectrum of PA are shown in Figure S10b, which clearly indicate that some excitation energy of KCDs is gained by PA which diminishes the emission property of KCDs is proved the inner filter effect causes the quenching. Further the possibility of hydrogen bonding between nitro group of PA and carboxyl moieties present on the surface of KCDs were confirmed by FT-IR analysis, (Figure S11) in which the peak assigned for carbonyl group (1760 cm−1) of KCDs was disappeared in presence of PA (KCDs-PA) and decrease in peak intensities of nitro group (1430–1610 cm−1) of PA with KCDs. Moreover, the disappearance of hydroxyl group at 3400 cm−1 has also suggested the hydrogen bonding between nitro group of PA and carboxyl/hydroxyl group of KCDs. Therefore the most possible quenching mechanism is an inner filter effect along with some non-specific electrostatic interactions like hydrogen bonding. Similar type of sensing mechanism was proposed in the literature [64].

Copper and iron are essential micronutrients for the proper functioning of organs and other metabolic processes [65]. According to Environmental Protection Agency of US, permissible limit of copper and iron in drinking water are 20 μM and ∼5.35 μM, respectively [66,67]. However, excess/decrease the level of these two elements may cause health related issues such as anemia, blood clotting, oxygen transport and protein denaturation, Alzheimer’s diseases and etc [68]. So, it is of importance to develop techniques that could reliable detect the level of these heavy metal ions in an aqueous medium.

Therefore, we have selected common metal ions such as Fe3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Ba2+ and Ni2+ and investigated the fluorescence response of KCDs in presence of these metal ions in water medium. Among the metal ions investigated, the fluorescence intensity of KCDs could be well quenched by Fe3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Ni2+ and Mn2+ is shown in Fig. 10 a. However, our ultimate aim is to detect the Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions in real water samples. In order to avoid the interferences from other metal ions, we have carried out measurements in different buffer medium [78] for the selective detection of metal ions such as Fe3+ and Cu2+. It is observed that Cu2+ shows more quenching efficiency in TRIS buffer medium at pH = 7.0, compared to the other metal ions (Fig. 10b). Similarly, the efficient quenching by Fe3+ was observed in NaAc-HAc buffer medium at pH = 7.0 (Fig. 10c). The selective detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in corresponding buffer solutions was attributed to the blanketing nature of the buffer solutions. As a result, compared to the other metal ions, Fe3+ and Cu2+ have stronger binding affinity and faster chelating kinetics with carboxylic groups on the surfaces of the KCDs especially in buffer medium. Further, the fluorescence titration method was performed by adding the different concentrations of metal ions into KCDs. Fig. 11 a & c shows the emission intensity of KCDs is quenched with increasing the concentration of Cu2+ in TRIS buffer (0–10 μM) and Fe3+ in NaAc-HAc buffer in the concentration range of 0–150 μM. The LOD was calculated (Fig. 11 b & d) for Cu2+ is 15.3 ng/ml and Fe3+ is 0.36 μg/ml at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Tables 3a and 3b
shows the detection limit of Fe3+ and Cu2+ using different materials through various methods [68–79] of detection which enlightens that these metals ions can be detected with high sensitivity at low concentration using fluorescence technique. The interference of other metal ions on the selective detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ is shown in Figure S12 a & b. In which the fluorescence intensity of KCDs in the presence of specific metal ion (Fe3+/Cu2+) as well as with interfering metal ions such as Pb2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Na+ and K+ indicated that KCDs with other interfering ions shows insignificant changes in the fluorescence intensity, at the same time a remarkable decrease in intensity in presence of a specific metal ion like Cu2+ or Fe3+ (KCDs with either Cu2+ or Fe3+ and with interfering metal ions).

Further, the detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions is performed in different kind of real water samples. The bar diagram shown in (Figure S13 a & b) clearly exposed that more or less same quenching efficiency of 20 μM of metals ions to KCDs fluorescence in all three types of water sample which is comparable with metal ions spiked in ultra pure (UP) water. This indicates that the practical use of this analysis in various kind of water samples. Table 4 shows the quantification for the selective detection of heavy metal ions in the presence of various interfering ions were in the range of 86.8%–115.4%. This scrutiny is obviously enlighten that KCDs is a promising fluorescent probe for detecting heavy metal ions like iron and copper in different kind of real water samples.

The mechanism behind the fluorescence quenching of KCDs was attributed to the interactions between carboxylate groups present in the KCDs surface and the metal ions (formation of non-fluorescent complexes) [80]. In order to confirm it we have adopted an experimental fluorescent upturn method using ethylene diamine tetra acetate (EDTA), a competitive chelator for metal ions. It is observed that the emission intensity of KCDs was decreased by adding metal ions which subsequently enhanced by adding EDTA proved that the metal ions involved in the quenching was undergoes a stable complexation with EDTA. Consequently the KCDs is now free from the complexation leads the enhancement/upturn in the quenched fluorescence [81] (Figure S14 a & b). From the above discussion the plausible quenching of KCDs by metal ion may due to, non-fluorescent complex formation in the excited state followed by electron transfer from the excited state of KCDs to the vacant d-orbitals of metal ions leads nonradiative electron/hole recombination [82].

In view of the effective utilization of by-product, viz., the undigested soot that got settled down at the bottom of the reaction vessel during the oxidative acid treatment was separated out as a byproduct through ultra centrifugation which was further washed with water, dried and calcined in an electrical furnace at 400 °C for 4 h. The final dried material (Fig. 12 a) was used as a filter bed for the purification of waste water collected from the kitchen garden which consist of mud, sand, salt, suspended and floating materials etc. The waste water was allowed to pass through the normal tissue paper as well as the tissue containing a layer of the calcined byproduct. The results shown in Fig. 12b illustrate that clear water can be obtained from the filter with calcined dried material as a filter bed and the pH of the filtered water was neutral. By using basic testing of water analysis it is observed that the level of alkalinity and organic impurities were lower in the filtered water. The extended results of waste water purification using the byproduct are underway in our laboratory which will be explored in near future. From this study it is suggested that instead of simply throwing the byproduct of any chemical reaction, one can think about the effective utilization of the same product for some other applications.

In conclusion, the kerosene fuel soot was successfully converted into nanostructured carbon dots via a one-step oxidative acid treatment. The KCDs particles were quasi-spherical in shape and average diameter of 5 nm which consist of various oxygen enclosed surface functionalities imparting excitation independent fluorescence behavior. The intermolecular interactions between the porphyrin and KCDs in aqueous medium were investigated and the possibility of electron injection from porphyrin to KCDs in the excited state has been confirmed through time resolved fluorescence decay measurements and rate of electron transfer has also been calculated. Nanosecond transient absorption studies were demonstrated the formation of porphyrin radical for the evidence of electron transfer process. On the other hand, the fluorescence of KCDs in water sensitively and selectively responded to picric acid by fluorescence quenching method in nanogram level and the sensing mechanism of inner filter effect was recognized. Moreover, KCDs was also applied for the detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions in different buffer medium with the detection limits of micro and nanogram level respectively. The sensing of metal ions as well as explosive was demonstrated in real water samples. Considering the excellent selectivity for the analytes, the fluorescent KCDs are expected to be applied as environmental surveillance in various fields. On the other hand, the unreacted soot has also been used as an affordable filter bed for the removal of alkaline and organic impurities from waste water which demonstrate 100% effective utilization of both the product as well as by-product towards sustainable environment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: V.S. thanks to the Department of Chemistry and BSACIST for the Junior Research Fellowship. M.A.J. thanks the Department of Science and Technology (DST), New Delhi, India for the DST-SERB Project (Ref. No. SB/FT/CS-125/2013, Dt. 30/06/2014). MAJ also thanks to SAIF, IIT Madras for assistance with NMR, HR-SEM and TCSPC facilities. MAJ also acknowledge to Ms. S. Thulasi, project fellow and Ms. G. Loganayagi, PG student for their assistance in soot collections and benchmark experiments. A.K. thanks NCUFP, University of Madras for spectral measurements.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.11.144.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
